name: Nereid
description: Nereids are sea nymphs in Greek mythology, daughters of the ancient sea god Nereus and the Oceanid Doris. They are typically depicted as beautiful maidens, often with flowing hair and adorned with sea-themed jewelry. Numbering fifty in most accounts, the Nereids embody the beauty and bounty of the sea. They are known for their benevolence and helpfulness to sailors in distress, often intervening to calm storms or guide ships to safety. Each Nereid possesses a unique name, often reflecting an aspect of the sea or their individual characteristics. They are associated with specific locations within the sea, such as bays, shores, and currents. While immortal, their power is generally limited to the marine environment. They are not as powerful as major sea deities like Poseidon, but their collective influence makes them significant figures in maritime lore. Nereids are often depicted riding dolphins, seahorses, or other sea creatures, further emphasizing their connection to the ocean.
subtitle: Sea nymph daughters of Nereus and Doris.
appearance: Beautiful maidens, typically depicted with long flowing hair, often adorned with sea-themed jewelry such as pearls, coral, and shells. They can sometimes appear with fish tails.
habitat: The Mediterranean Sea, Aegean Sea, and other bodies of water; particularly found near shores, bays, and currents.
behavior: Generally benevolent and helpful to sailors, they are known to guide ships, calm storms, and offer aid to those in distress. They are often depicted as playful and joyous, enjoying the beauty and freedom of the sea.
weaknesses: Loss of connection to the sea
Disrespect or pollution of the ocean

keyMyths.title: Achilles and Thetis
keyMyths.description: The Nereid Thetis was the mother of the hero Achilles. She attempted to make him immortal as an infant by dipping him in the River Styx, but failed to fully submerge his heel, leaving it vulnerable. Her efforts to protect her son are a central theme in the Iliad.

keyMyths.title: Perseus and Andromeda
keyMyths.description: Cassiopeia, queen of Ethiopia, boasted that her daughter Andromeda was more beautiful than the Nereids. This angered Poseidon, who sent a sea monster to ravage the land. Andromeda was offered as a sacrifice but was rescued by Perseus. This tale shows the Nereids' capacity for vengeance when their beauty or status is challenged.

keyMyths.title: Arion and the Dolphin
keyMyths.description: The musician Arion was thrown overboard a ship by pirates who wanted his wealth. A group of dolphins, charmed by his music, rescued him and carried him to shore. Some accounts attribute this rescue to the intervention of the Nereids, who favored Arion's artistic talent.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Provides significant details regarding the Nereid Thetis and her role as the mother of Achilles.
sources.text: Provides significant details regarding the Nereid Thetis and her role as the mother of Achilles.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Lists the names and parentage of the fifty Nereids, providing a foundational understanding of their genealogy.
sources.text: Lists the names and parentage of the fifty Nereids, providing a foundational understanding of their genealogy.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: Contains various myths involving Nereids, including the story of Andromeda and Cassiopeia's insult to the sea nymphs.
sources.text: Contains various myths involving Nereids, including the story of Andromeda and Cassiopeia's insult to the sea nymphs.
